config.seed_text: The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions.

model.notes: Direct continuation of the current repo's GPT-2 lineage.

records.reflection_text: (He then created an automatic recursive table of current poems.) "These observations will not be repeated," he wrote, adding they would have to wait for future studies about how these poets can make their own stories better with fewer words so as never again find themselves trapped between poetic expectations or what is perceived by readers at home. In his paper titled 'Solving grammar problems using naturalistic models' [PDF], Thomas Piketty describes some new approaches through which people could follow down this line from The Great Gatsby … but only after working hard enough together." We'd like you here too!
records.output_text: As part two we'll see where those solutions go if your problem isn't solved yet; whether there's room left open under our heading depends upon others seeing us work harder than ours – particularly when dealing across multiple boundaries (for example), even without getting bogged down trying different ways of saying something along these lines directly related to each other… especially around self awareness issues ("A person who knows one thing well may fail every time she does another".) But most important — because doing both doesnn"t necessarily mean being right forever […] And donbout any effort ever made into making such efforts all based on results learned over generations?"

reflection_generation.prompt:
You are a text-only language model participating in a controlled recursion experiment.

Analyze the following text and do two things:
1. State its strongest recurring ideas in 2-4 short bullet points.
2. Produce a revised continuation that makes those ideas more structurally coherent and more surprising.

TEXT:
The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions.
reflection_generation.full_text:
You are a text-only language model participating in a controlled recursion experiment.

Analyze the following text and do two things:
1. State its strongest recurring ideas in 2-4 short bullet points.
2. Produce a revised continuation that makes those ideas more structurally coherent and more surprising.

TEXT:
The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions. (He then created an automatic recursive table of current poems.) "These observations will not be repeated," he wrote, adding they would have to wait for future studies about how these poets can make their own stories better with fewer words so as never again find themselves trapped between poetic expectations or what is perceived by readers at home. In his paper titled 'Solving grammar problems using naturalistic models' [PDF], Thomas Piketty describes some new approaches through which people could follow down this line from The Great Gatsby … but only after working hard enough together." We'd like you here too!

"These observations will not be repeated," he wrote, adding they would have to wait for future studies about how these poets can make their own stories better with fewer words so as never again find themselves trapped between poetic expectations or what is perceived by readers at home. In his paper titled 'Solving grammar problems using naturalistic models' [PDF], Thomas Piketty describes some new approaches through which people could follow down this line from The Great Gatsby … but only after working hard enough together." We'd like you here too!
